title: Sampha Came Back and It Felt Like Running Into an Old Friend
author: Allastair Voss
body:
[spotify:artist/2WoVwexZuODvclzULjPQtm]

## Six Years Is a Long Time

I remember where I was when Process came out. I know that sounds like the kind of thing people say about records that changed their life, and I'm saying it precisely because of that , because Sampha's debut felt like an event in the quietest possible way, a record that didn't announce itself but arrived and then refused to leave. And then he went away. For six years, or close to it, he was almost entirely absent from music. When Lahai appeared in late 2023 I felt something that surprised me: not excitement exactly, but relief.

Lahai is named for Sampha's grandfather. It's an album about grief and fatherhood and continuity, about the way the dead stay present in the living. These are enormous themes and Sampha handles them with a lightness that shouldn't work but does , a gentleness that keeps the weight of the material from becoming crushing. He has that ability, very rare in any artist, to make intimacy feel like an achievement rather than an exposure.

The production here has expanded. There are strings, electronics, rhythmic structures that reference West African music , his parents are Sierra Leonean and that lineage is more explicitly present here than on Process. But the expansion never crowds out the essential quality, which is Sampha's voice. It remains one of the more astonishing instruments in contemporary music: technically precise, emotionally unguarded, capable of suggesting an entire interiority in a single phrase.

## What Grief Does to Music

Process was haunted by his mother's death. Lahai is haunted differently , by fatherhood, by new life, by the way becoming a parent puts you in conversation with all the generations before you. There's a track here that moves me every time I hear it, where the piano part feels like it's being played in a room adjacent to sorrow rather than inside it. That kind of emotional adjacency , not wallowing, not avoiding, just being near the feeling , is extraordinarily difficult to achieve and Sampha does it almost casually.

I do think about the six years. Artists disappear for all kinds of reasons , grief, creative block, the simple desire to live a life outside of public scrutiny. Sampha's absence never felt like withdrawal in a dramatic sense; he was occasionally mentioned, occasionally collaborated, just wasn't making his own work visible. The return doesn't feel triumphant. It feels considered. Like he needed to become the person who could make this particular record before he could make it.

There's a lesson in there somewhere about the relationship between living and making, though I'm wary of making it too tidy. Artists aren't obligated to produce. The time between things is its own kind of work. What Lahai suggests is that the distance was purposeful , that something accumulated in those years that couldn't have been forced into existence any sooner.

## Voice and What It Carries

Sampha's voice is one of those instruments that critics have difficulty writing about precisely because it does too many things at once. The technical description , the falsetto range, the control, the restraint , captures the mechanics but not the effect. What the voice does emotionally is suggest that everything the song is about is already happening inside the person singing before a single word is delivered. The emotional content precedes the lyrical content. By the time he opens a line you already feel what the line is about.

This is a quality of gospel singing that Sampha absorbed from the church tradition and has redirected into secular contexts. The voice as vessel rather than vehicle. The voice as something that carries more than it could reasonably carry through technique alone. Process demonstrated this. Lahai confirms it has deepened. The quality that was present on his debut , the sense of a voice speaking from somewhere more interior than vocalism usually reaches , has not diminished with time. It's heavier with experience, but the weight adds dimension rather than burden.

The Sierra Leonean lineage that Lahai makes explicit is also present in the voice itself. The rhythmic patterns his ancestors sang are in how he phrases. This is not ornamental diversity; it is the actual substrate of the sound.



## On Six Years of Silence

The six years between Process and Lahai are not the longest hiatus in music history but they feel significant because of what Process was. A debut that complete, that assured, that emotionally devastating , it raised the question of where you go from there with unusual urgency. The answer turned out to be: into a life. Marriage, fatherhood, loss, the accumulation of experience that requires time rather than studio hours. Lahai is the record those six years produced, and it could not have been made before those six years were lived.

## The Quiet Ones

Sampha occupies an interesting position in contemporary British music , beloved by other musicians, critically revered, but not quite a mainstream presence in the way that other artists of his caliber might be. He doesn't have the crossover obviousness of some of his peers. He exists in a space that requires a certain kind of listening, a willingness to sit with something that unfolds slowly.

I keep thinking about the fact that he played piano on 'Saint Pablo.' That Drake called him for features. That his vocal credit appears across a decade of music that wouldn't be what it is without him. And yet the solo work is so much more interior than any of that suggests , so much more concerned with the private rather than the public, the personal rather than the spectacular.

Running into an old friend is the right metaphor, I think. You pick up where you left off, and realize the time away doesn't diminish anything. It might have deepened it.

Lahai will settle into Sampha's discography as the record that proved the return was worth waiting for , that the silence wasn't stasis but development, that the person who came back was capable of something the earlier work couldn't have made. That's the best possible outcome for an extended absence. That's what happened.

The old friend, encountered again. The time away present in how you greet each other, in what's changed and what hasn't. The conversation picking up somewhere you didn't expect but recognizing immediately as right.
